  You will see one candidate hero image. Score the SUBJECT of

  title. Be strict — keyword overlap is not relevance. A bee

  pudding; a roast cut is not a Beef Wellington.

TIERS
  EXACT   — Image unambiguously shows the named subject.
            For a recipe: the finished dish (or its key visible
            stage — bread dough being kneaded for a bread
            tutorial, lambs nursing for a lambing tutorial).

            its characteristic output (a treble crochet stitch
            in close-up, a finished joint, a soldered seam).

            (a bottle of syrup, a balm tin, a poultice on the

entries.tutorialSubtitle: The undulating wave pattern produced by a paired comb pass on a nonpareil base
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The Spanish wave is a flowing S-curve pattern that fills the sheet in repeating vertical bands. Like the bouquet, it begins with a nonpareil base, but the final pass uses a wide comb drawn in alternating left-right zigzag strokes down the length of the tray rather than a single straight stroke. The result is an undulating wave that crosses the full width of the sheet.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: The pressure-and-release mechanics of the pointed nib
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Copperplate uses a flexible pointed nib that produces thick strokes through pressure and thin strokes by releasing that pressure. The thick-and-thin logic is entirely different from the broad-edge nib: there is no nib-angle geometry, only pressure control. This tutorial establishes the five basic strokes that underlie the entire Copperplate alphabet.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: One of the two primary origami bases, used in the waterbomb and balloon models
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The waterbomb base is one of the two foundational collapse structures in origami (the other is the preliminary base). It is formed by collapsing a square with two perpendicular valley folds and one diagonal mountain fold into a flat triangle. The base is used directly in the waterbomb and balloon and as an intermediate step in many more complex models.

The result is a translucent decorative paper useful for lampshades, greeting cards, and pressed-flower archives.

entries.tutorialTitle: Navajo plying (chain plying) from a single cop
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Making a balanced 3-ply yarn from a single cop using the chain-ply loop method
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Navajo plying (also called chain plying) produces a 3-ply yarn from a single cop of singles by chaining loops through each other as the singles ply. The technique preserves colour sequences in variegated yarn, produces a balanced 3-ply without a second cop, and is useful for small quantities where spinning two matching cops is impractical. The finished yarn is identical in structure to a conventional 3-ply.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Ultra-thin felted fabric made from a single wisp-layer of fine merino or Corriedale
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cobweb felt is felted fabric so thin it is translucent when held to the light. A single whisper-thin layer of fine merino or Corriedale is laid, wetted out, and felted until the fibres lock into a stable gossamer cloth. The technique is used for scarves, lampshades, and artwork overlays. The critical skill is laying an even web thin enough to felt through but without holes.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Applying raised lines of slip from a squeeze bottle
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Slip trailing is a decoration technique in which fluid clay slip is squeezed through a small nozzle in continuous lines onto the clay surface, building up a raised pattern. On air-dry clay, the trailed slip dries with the piece and is sealed as part of the finished surface. A plastic squeeze bottle with a fine nozzle is the standard tool. No kiln or wheel is needed.
